About Callum I work in security, with current focus on Web3 and a longer-term interest in AI security. My background is in binary analysis, reverse engineering, and application security. I use this site to keep notes, writeups, and working documents in one place. You must not fool yourself, and you are the easiest person to fool. EducationM.S. in Artificial Intelligence · B.S. in Computer Science & Mathematics. Current directionSecurity first, active work in Web3, longer-term interest in AI security. Tech focus Security Binary analysisReverse engineeringFuzzingWeb securityExploitation Web3 Protocol securitySmart contract auditingEVMFoundrySolanaMove AI & Systems AI securityLLM agentsPyTorchMulti-agent systemsAutomated reasoning Direction The work is grounded in security, but the areas of focus keep moving. Security This is still the core of my work, especially low-level systems, reverse engineering, and attack surface analysis. Web3 Most of my current work is here: protocol risk, smart contract assumptions, and review practice. AI This is the longer-term direction, especially around agents, tool use, model behavior, and system security. Approach This is a working site, not a polished portfolio. I want the material here to stay useful: clear enough to revisit, specific enough to reproduce, and organized well enough to build on later. If something is published here, it should be worth coming back to. Principles Write clearly enough for future me to trust and reuse the note. Keep assumptions, environment, and evidence close to the work. Prefer systems that can be inspected all the way down. How To Read This Site Start from the map, then move into the areas you care about. DocsStart here if you want the top-level map.SecurityReverse engineering, pwn, browser, IoT, Apple platform, and general security notes.Web3Protocol study, audit practice, and review notes.AINotes on models, agents, tools, and the road toward AI security.BlogMore finished writing.

This blog is for writing that deserves a more public form than the notes in the docs section. Most of the site is a working research base: narrower notes, study material, references, and write-ups kept close to the original work. The blog is where that material gets distilled into something more readable for a broader audience. I expect most posts here to fall into a few categories: research summaries worth reading on their own vulnerability analysis and case studies write-ups on methods, tooling, and review practice occasional reflections on how I approach technical work The standard is simple: explain the problem clearly keep examples concrete leave the reader with something reusable The goal is not to publish often. The goal is to publish things worth revisiting.

This site collects notes and research across security, Web3, and AI. It is organized as a working knowledge base rather than a finished archive. Some sections are already taking shape; others are still being sorted into cleaner tracks. The goal is simple: keep useful material close to the original work, then turn it into notes that stay readable and reusable later.
The site has two main layers: Security / Web3 / AI Domain-based notes, study material, and research records. Blog More polished public writing.

Security: reverse engineering, pwn, browser, Apple platform work, IoT, and broader security notes Web3: protocol security, contract review, audit thinking, and ecosystem-specific study AI: model behavior, agent systems, tool use, prompt injection, and AI security questions Templates: writing formats used across the site
Notes stay close to the domain where they belong. As a section grows, it gets split into narrower subdirectories. Material that becomes worth sharing more broadly can move into blog/.
date-based notes: yyyy-mm-dd-topic.md topic notes: area-topic.md writeups: platform-challenge-name.md
Sections are still being organized and expanded. Some directories are broad on purpose and will narrow over time. The emphasis is on keeping the material useful, technical, and easy to revisit.

AI security is the longer research direction of this site. This section gathers notes on model behavior, agent systems, tool use, prompt injection, and the security questions that appear when AI is embedded into real products. Current topics include: prompt injection / jailbreak tool use / agent safety memory, retrieval, MCP, and browser automation risks model behavior analysis red teaming methods AI product security review The material here is still being sorted into a cleaner structure. Some pages are foundational; others are narrower notes tied to specific attack surfaces or system designs.

This section holds the concepts that make later AI security work easier to follow. It is the right place for notes on models, agents, tools, retrieval, trust boundaries, and the system assumptions behind AI-native attack surfaces.

Security is the center of gravity of this site. This section holds the broader security work that does not belong exclusively to Web3 or AI. It includes notes from binary analysis, reverse engineering, application security, and general attack-surface work. Topics here include: web security browser security system security vulnerability analysis exploitation thinking defensive mechanisms and detection ideas The structure is still being cleaned up. For now, the section is grouped into a few core tracks: Foundations Web Reverse Pwn Browser IoT Apple Cryptography Misc

Apple platform security work across macOS and iOS is collected here. It covers app architecture, sandboxing, entitlements, IPC, persistence, update flows, and the platform details that matter when analyzing software in the Apple ecosystem.

iOS security notes and platform-specific research are collected here. It covers app behavior, mobile trust boundaries, signing and entitlement models, runtime analysis, and the practical security properties of the iOS ecosystem.

This section focuses on macOS-specific application security and platform behavior. Topics here include app boundaries, sandboxing, entitlements, IPC, persistence, updater behavior, and local trust assumptions in real software.
Browser security notes and research are collected here. It covers renderer boundaries, IPC surfaces, extension risk, sandbox assumptions, and browser-facing internals.

Cryptography notes from a security engineering perspective are collected here. It covers primitives, protocol reasoning, applied cryptography, implementation mistakes, and the gap between theoretical guarantees and deployed systems.

This section holds the concepts and mental models that support everything else in the security track. Notes here usually deal with threat modeling, attack surface analysis, trust boundaries, primitives, debugging habits, and other ideas that keep appearing across domains.

IoT and embedded security notes are collected here. It collects material on firmware analysis, device attack surfaces, protocol behavior, hardware work, and the security assumptions that appear in connected systems. Current tracks: Hardware Security Firmware Automotive Protocols Case Studies
Vehicle-related security notes are collected here, especially around connected systems, in-vehicle communication, control assumptions, and modern automotive attack surfaces.

Concrete IoT security case studies are collected here. It keeps examples worth studying closely: vulnerable products, design failures, exploitation chains, and the defensive lessons that come out of them.

Firmware analysis, extraction workflows, emulation, patching, and the assumptions embedded in device software are collected here. Notes here include unpacking, filesystem inspection, embedded Linux behavior, update logic, and firmware-specific vulnerability patterns.

Notes on physical interfaces, hardware trust boundaries, debug surfaces, chip-level assumptions, and the security properties below the software layer belong here. Topics here include JTAG, UART, secure boot assumptions, hardware-backed storage, fault models, and teardown-driven analysis.

Communication protocols that matter in device security are collected here. Notes here cover protocol design, trust assumptions, parsing behavior, transport boundaries, and the ways protocol-level weaknesses become exploitable in real systems.
This section keeps material that is useful but not yet settled into a clearer track. Some notes land here first and move later once a stronger pattern emerges.

Binary exploitation, memory corruption, and exploit-development notes are collected here. It covers primitives, mitigations, exploitation patterns, debugging traces, and the kinds of step-by-step material that benefit from careful writeup.

Reverse engineering notes, workflows, and case studies are collected here. It is where work on binary analysis, tooling, patch diffing, static and dynamic analysis, and the process of understanding unfamiliar code is collected.

Web security notes and case-driven analysis are collected here. It covers both fundamentals and recurring problem areas: authentication, authorization, SSRF, deserialization, client-side issues, browser-facing attack surfaces, and the reasoning behind common exploitation paths.

This section contains the writing formats used across the site. They are meant to make new pages easier to start without forcing everything into one shape. Available formats: Markdown template MDX template Research note template Writeup template Web3 review template The formats stay practical: scope, environment, versions, findings, references, and follow-up questions are easy to capture when they matter.

Web3 is the area currently receiving the most focused study. This section collects notes on contract security, protocol design, audit practice, and case studies from real systems. The material here includes: Solidity / Move / Rust contract study notes audit checklists vulnerability pattern summaries protocol design review attack case studies The structure is still being refined. As more material accumulates, it will separate more clearly by protocol, ecosystem, and vulnerability class.
This section holds the concepts that support later Web3 security work. It is where notes on execution models, protocol assumptions, transaction flow, and recurring vulnerability classes belong.

This section collects Web3-focused writeups from hands-on challenges, labs, and protocol exercises. The goal is to keep concise notes on solved material, including the vulnerable assumption, the exploitation path, and the key lesson worth remembering in a Web3 context. Current tracks include: Ethernaut

This directory is for notes on Ethernaut levels that have already been completed. Official site: ethernaut.openzeppelin.com Each writeup can record: the level objective the vulnerable design or assumption the exploitation path the lesson that generalizes beyond the challenge As individual levels are solved and written up, they can be added here as separate notes.

Source: ethernaut.openzeppelin.com/level/0 Hello Ethernaut is the onboarding level, but it already teaches the habit that matters for the rest of the game: stop trusting the UI, open the console, and read what the contract is actually exposing. This is not a real exploit challenge yet. It is Ethernaut teaching you how it wants to be played.

If the page opens in read-only mode, Ethernaut cannot see a wallet yet. The fix is straightforward: install MetaMask, connect it, switch to Sepolia, and fund the account with a bit of test ETH. You do not need a full wallet tutorial here. The only parts that matter for this level are: MetaMask is installed and unlocked Sepolia is selected the account has enough test ETH to pay for gas To get to Sepolia, enable hidden test networks first, then switch networks from the wallet home screen. One small point that trips people up the first time: your address is usually the same across Ethereum mainnet and Sepolia. What changes is the network state, not the address itself. Balances, transactions, and contracts are separate per network.

Most Sepolia faucets are fine until they are not. The main annoyance is anti-abuse logic: some faucets refuse fresh wallets, some want a logged-in account, and some check for a small amount of mainnet ETH. The practical fix is simple: try a normal faucet first if it rejects the wallet, switch to a fallback if the wallet is brand new, a browser-based Sepolia PoW faucet is often the path of least resistance Useful links: Alchemy faucets Google Cloud Web3 Sepolia faucet Coinbase Developer Platform faucets Infura faucet Sepolia PoW faucet

Once the wallet is connected, open the browser console. That is where Ethernaut stops being a web page and starts behaving like a contract playground. You should immediately see the level banner, helper text, and the current level address. The first helper to check is player: player That gives you the connected account address. Then confirm the account actually has gas: await getBalance(player) And finally, ask Ethernaut what helpers it exposes: help() That one command tells you almost everything about the working surface of the platform: player address, current level address, current instance, contract wrapper, and a few convenience utilities.
documents.s: The Console Is the Real Interface

documents.t: At this point there are really only two objects worth caring about.

This is the global game contract wrapper. It is not the challenge instance itself. It is the thing that manages levels and validation. ethernaut await ethernaut.owner() This is mostly orientation. The point is to get used to the fact that the game objects are normal contract wrappers, not magical UI-only abstractions.
This is the current level instance once you have requested one. For actual solving, this is the object that matters.
First, approve the wallet connection request from Ethernaut. If MetaMask asks about network permissions, approve Sepolia.
Click Get New Instance and approve the transaction in MetaMask. After deployment, Ethernaut prints the instance address in the console.

This level is basically a guided tour through the contract interface. The contract keeps telling you where to go next, and the only real job is to pay attention. The interaction chain is: await contract.info() await contract.info1() await contract.info2('hello') await contract.infoNum() await contract.info42() await contract.theMethodName() await contract.method7123949() await contract.password() await contract.authenticate('ethernaut0') There is nothing subtle here. Ethernaut is teaching you to read the contract instead of poking at buttons and hoping for the best.
Once the interaction chain is complete, hit Submit Instance and approve the transaction. If everything is correct, Ethernaut marks the level as solved.

After completion, Ethernaut reveals the source. That is where the level becomes worth reflecting on. Here is the same contract with commentary added inline: // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^0.8.0; contract Instance { // Declaring this as public means Solidity automatically creates password(). // In other words, the "secret" is readable by anyone. string public password; // This is a breadcrumb. The value 42 is meant to push the player toward info42(). uint8 public infoNum = 42; // Another breadcrumb. Because it is public, the player can also read it directly. string public theMethodName = "The method name is method7123949."; // This is the real completion state for the level. bool private cleared = false; // constructor // The level instance is deployed with an initial password. constructor(string memory _password) { password = _password; } // First hint in the chain. function info() public pure returns (string memory) { return "You will find what you need in info1()."; } // Second hint in the chain. function info1() public pure returns (string memory) { return 'Try info2(), but with "hello" as a parameter.'; } // The first place where the player has to pass an input. // Only "hello" unlocks the next clue. function info2(string memory param) public pure returns (string memory) { if (keccak256(abi.encodePacked(param)) == keccak256(abi.encodePacked("hello"))) { return "The property infoNum holds the number of the next info method to call."; } return "Wrong parameter."; } // infoNum points here. function info42() public pure returns (string memory) { return "theMethodName is the name of the next method."; } // theMethodName points here. function method7123949() public pure returns (string memory) { return "If you know the password, submit it to authenticate()."; } // If the supplied value matches password, the level is cleared. function authenticate(string memory passkey) public { if (keccak256(abi.encodePacked(passkey)) == keccak256(abi.encodePacked(password))) { cleared = true; } } // Helper to check whether the level has been solved. function getCleared() public view returns (bool) { return cleared; } } The flow is deliberately simple. One function points to the next until the contract finally tells you to read the password and pass it into authenticate(). The real punchline is this line: string public password; That is the whole lesson. The contract frames password like a secret, but the moment it is declared public, Solidity exposes it through an auto-generated getter. It is not hidden, protected, or private in any meaningful sense. You can read it directly and feed it back into authenticate(). That is why this level matters even though it is easy: it forces you to notice the difference between how something is described in the UI and what the contract actually exposes on-chain.
documents.s: What the Source Code Makes Obvious

documents.t: The obvious lesson is "public state is public". But there is a slightly broader point underneath it: treat the contract interface as the source of truth do not assume a value is secret just because it sounds secret read outputs carefully because contracts often reveal the next step in plain sight separate what is hidden in the frontend from what is hidden on-chain That is a real smart contract review habit, not just a game mechanic.
documents.s: What This Level Is Really Teaching

documents.t: This level is easy on purpose, but the workflow it teaches is exactly the workflow that keeps showing up in Ethernaut: connect a wallet get onto the right network fund the account inspect the console helpers read the public interface create an instance interact with the contract submit the result Once that loop feels normal, the later levels become much less awkward.
This level is done once you can: connect a wallet successfully get onto Sepolia with enough gas create a level instance follow the contract's public hint chain submit the solved instance

This is onboarding, not exploitation. The browser console is part of the game, not an optional extra. Public variables are not secrets. Reading the contract is usually more productive than guessing from the UI. Getting your wallet, network, and faucet setup right saves a lot of time later.
MetaMask: How to install MetaMask Alchemy faucets Google Cloud Web3 Sepolia faucet Coinbase Developer Platform faucets Infura faucet Sepolia PoW faucet
